California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


November 12, 1985


Ralph J. Gampell
Director
Administrative Office of the Courts
State Building, Room 3154
350 McAllister Street
San Francisco, CA 94102

Re:  Conflict of Interest Code for
Judicial Council of
California Your Request for
Advice
Our File No. A-85-211

Dear Mr. Gampell:

This letter is sent to respond to your request for advice
and to confirm my conversation with Ben McClinton of your staff
concerning the conflict of interest code for the Judicial
Council of California.

The code was proposed in response to the adoption of
SB 1427 (Ch. 727, Stats. 1984) which amended the Political
Reform Act and applied the financial disclosure and
disqualification provisions of the Act to all judicial
agencies.1/  You have asked pursuant to Section 83114(b)
whether the proposed code and subsequent amendments to it
comply with the political Reform Act.

Before I confirm the advice I have given Mr. McClinton on
the specific contents of the code, I would like to note that it

_____________________
1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  The relevant
provisions for this letter are Government Code Sections 82019,
82048, 82049, 87100-103, and 87300-313.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
indicated.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Ralph J. Gampell
November 12, 1985
Page 2


is our view that the Judicial Council may be entitled to
request an exemption from the financial disclosure provisions
of the Act because the decisions of the Judicial Council do not
generally affect specific private financial interests.
However, it is our understanding that the Judicial Council does
not wish to seek an exemption.

In the proposed code, a transactional disclosure category
is set forth for the attorney members of the Council.2/ This
means that, on their annual financial disclosure statements,
these members will certify, under penalty of perjury, that they
have not made, or participated in, any Judicial Council
decision in which they had a financial interest within the
meaning of Sections 87100 and 87103. This form of disclosure
is appropriate where the potential economic interests which
could be affected are varied and diverse but it is highly
unlikely that any one economic interest will be affected. See
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18733. This reasoning is applicable
to the Judicial Council.

The code also provides that any disclosure required of the
attorney-members by the code shall be made in accordance with
Business and Professions Code Section 6036. This comports with
Section 82048 as amended by SB 1427 which provides:

"Public official" means every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency, but does not include judges and court
commissioners in the judicial branch of government.
"Public official" also does not include members of the
Board of Governors and designated employees of the
State Bar of California, members of the Judicial
Council, and members of the Commission on Judicial
Performance, provided that they are subject to the
provisions of Article 2.5 (commencing with Section
6035) of Chapter 4 of Division 3 of the Business and
Professions Code as provided in section 6038 of that
article.

In summary, it is our opinion that the code as proposed and
adopted by the Judicial Council complies with the letter and
spirit of the Political Reform Act. If I can be of further

_________________
2/ The judge and legislator members of the Council are
already covered directly by Sections 87200, et seq., and are
thus not covered by the code.

Ralph J. Gampell
November 12, 1985
Page 3


assistance, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.
We are also happy to work with you and your staff further on
the development of a satisfactory disclosure form.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Diane Maura Fishburn
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

DMF:plh

CHIEF JUSTICE
ROSE ELIZABETH BIRD
CHAIRPERSON

JUDICIAL COUNCIL OF CALIFORNIA

ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICE OF THE COURTS
STATE BUILDING, ROOM 3154
350 McALLISTER STREET, SAN FRANCISCO 94102 • (415) 557-3203

RALPH J GAMPELL
DIRECTOR

BURTON W OLIVER
CHIEF DEPUTY DIRECTOR

LEGISLATIVE OFFICE
100 LIBRARY AND COURTS BUILDING, SACRAMENTO 95814 • (916) 445-7524

October 8, 1985

Mr. Dan Stanford
Chairman
Fair Political Practices
Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Dear Mr. Stanford:

On August 12 we sent you a draft conflict of interest
code for Judicial Council members for commission comment under
Government Code sections 83113 and 87312. Since then several
proposals for amendments to the draft code have been considered
by our staff. (A copy of the original draft code is enclosed.)

We request written advice under section 83114(b) of
the Government Code on the following amendments to sections 2
and 3 of the draft Judicial Council code (additions are underlined; deletions are struck over):

Section 2. Disclosure of financial interests

Members shall disclose an investments, interests in
real property, or and income if during a reporting period a
decision of the Judicial Council materially affects the
investment, interest, or income. The information disclosed
shall be that as required by sections 87206 and 87207 of the
Government Code, except that members subject to article 2.5 of
chapter 4 of division 3 of the Business and Professions Code
shall disclose the information required by section 6036 of the
Business and Professions Code. This code does not require disclosure by any member who files a statement under article 2 of
chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act of 1974, Government Code
section 87200 et seq.

Mr. Dan Stanford                    2                    October 8, 1985


Section 3.    Contents of disclosure statements and time of
              filing statements and reporting period

       (a)  [initial statements]  Each person who is a member on
the date this code is first approved by the Supreme Court shall
file a statement identifying the member within 30 days of
approval.

       (b)  [Statements on assuming office]  Each person who
becomes a member after the effective date of this code shall
file a statement identifying the member within 30 days after
taking the oath of office.

       (c)  [Annual statements]  Each member shall file an annual
statement by March 1.  The statement shall disclose the
information required in section 2 for the period following the
closing date of the last statement required to be filed.

       (d)  [Statements after leaving office]  Each former member
shall file a statement within 30 days after leaving office.
The statement shall disclose the information required by section
2 for the period between the closing date of the last
statement required to be filed and the date of leaving office.

       Each member subject to article 2.5 of chapter 4 of
division 3 of the Business and Professions Code shall file
annual statements disclosing whether or not the member during
the reporting period was required to disqualify himself or her-
self under Business and Professions Code section 6036 from mak-
ing or participating in making or attempting to influence any
decisions of the Judicial Council.  If disqualification was
required during the reporting period, the statement shall
identify the decisions requiring disqualification and report
whether the member complied with subdivision (d) of Business
and Professions Code section 6036.  Annual statements shall
cover the period from February 1 through January 31 and shall
be filed by March 1.  The first annual statement shall cover
the period since assuming office.  The final statement shall be
filed within 30 days after leaving office and cover the period
from the closing date of the most recent statement filed to the
date of leaving office.

Mr. Dan Stanford                    3                    October 8, 1985


          A copy of a staff memorandum giving reasons for the
proposed amendments is enclosed.

          We would appreciate an early response.  A reply by
October 15 would allow time to prepare and mail a report to the
council members before committee meetings on November 2.

                                   Very truly yours,

                                   [signature]

                                   Ralph J. Gampell
                                   Director

RJG/bt
5140k

Enclosures

[DRAFT]

(Submitted Aug. 12, 1985)

CONFLICT OF INTEREST CODE
FOR THE
JUDICIAL COUNCIL OF CALIFORNIA

Section 1. Enumeration of decision-making positions
All members of the Judicial Council are subject to
this code.

Section 2. Disclosure of financial interests
Members shall disclose an investment, interest in real
property, or income if during a reporting period a decision of
the Judicial Council materially affects the investment, inter-
est, or income. The information disclosed shall be that re-
quired by sections 87206 and 87207 of the Government Code.
This code does not require disclosure by any member who files a
statement under article 2 of chapter 7 of the Political Reform
Act of 1974, Government Code section 87200 et seq.

Section 3. Time of filing statements and reporting period
(a) [Initial statements] Each person who is a member on
the date this code is first approved by the Supreme Court shall
file a statement identifying the member within 30 days of
approval.
(b) [Statements on assuming office] Each person who
becomes a member after the effective date of this code shall
file a statement identifying the member within 30 days after
taking the oath of office.
(c) [Annual statements] Each member shall file an annual
statement by March 1. The statement shall disclose the infor-
mation required in section 2 for the period following the
closing date of the last statement required to be filed.

(d) [Statements after leaving office] Each former member
shall file a statement within 30 days after leaving office.
The statement shall disclose the information required by sec-
tion 2 for the period between the closing date of the last
statement required to be filed and the date of leaving office.

Section 4. Place of filing statements
Members shall file the statements required by section
2 with the Secretary of the Judicial Council (Administrative
Director of the Courts) on forms prescribed by the Fair Poli-
tical Practices Commission.

Section 5. Assistance to members
A member may request assistance concerning duties
under this code from the Fair Political Practices Commission
under section 83114 of the Government Code.

Section 6. Definitions
The definitions in the Political Reform Act of 1974,
Government Code section 81000 et seq., are incorporated by
reference in this code.

2
4546k

TO:    Ralph J. Gampell
       Director

FROM:  Ben McClinton
       Staff Attorney

DATE:  October 2, 1985

SUBJECT: Amendment to Draft Judicial Council
         Conflict of Interest Code

       Attorney members of the Judicial Council have ex-
pressed concern over the disclosure requirements of the coun-
cil's draft conflict of interest code.  As you recall, the
draft code covers all council members, but only attorney mem-
bers appointed by the State Bar of California have any active
disclosure obligations imposed on them.

       Judge and legislator members of the council are al-
ready filing disclosure statements (form 721) for interests
held throughout the state as required by Government Code sec-
tion 87200 et seq.1/  Members who file under section 87200 et
seq. are relieved from filing under any conflict of interest
code since their statements amount to the fullest disclosure
possible under any code.  (§ 87302(b), see also Cal. Admin.
Code, tit. 2, § 18730(b)(3).)


Transactional disclosure in the draft code

       The draft code adopted a "transactional disclosure"
procedure for council members.  This procedure is intended to
apply to persons included in codes as "designated employees"
(§ 82019) whose decisions may conceivably affect a broad range
of financial interests, but identification of the specific
types of interests that "may foreseeably be affected materially by any decision" of the employee is not possible.  (See
§ 87302(a).)  Decisions made by the council affect broad areas
of court administration, practice, and procedure; the specific

_____________________________
1/  Unless otherwise noted, all code references are to the
    Government Code.

types of interests that may be affected are not "foreseeable."2/

Section 2 of the council's original draft code required disclosure of a specific investment, interest in real property, or income "if during a reporting period a decision of the Judicial Council materially affects the investment, interest, or income." The requirement that attorney members of the council specifically identify the financial interest (including the source of income) appears to exceed the intent of the legislation that brought the Judicial Council and other judicial branch agencies under the Political Reform Act of 1974. (Stats. 1984, ch. 727. A copy is attached at pp. 8-11.)

Attorney members under the legislation

The legislation bringing judicial branch agencies into the Political Reform Act makes special provision for attorney members of the Judicial Council, the Commission on Judicial Performance, and the State Bar's Board of Governors and designated employees. The special provisions affect both disqualification and disclosure.

The most sweeping provision excludes these attorney members from the definition of "public official" (§ 82048) provided they are subject to the disqualification and disclosure requirements of Business and Professions Code sections 6035-6038. This exclusion removes attorney members of these bodies from the central provision of the act -- the general prohibition on public officials against conflicts of interest

2/ All foreseeable decisions relating to contracting, purchasing, etc. for Judicial Council operations have been delegated to the Administrative Director of the Courts under the authority granted by section 6, article VI of the Constitution. The persons making those decisions will be designated in a separate code for the Administrative Office of the Courts and the types of financial interests affected will be identified there.

2
0406H

(§ 87100). Business and Professions Code section 6038,3/
however, specifically makes these attorney members subject to
the State Bar's general prohibition against conflicts in sec-
tion 6036 (copy attached at p. 12).

The intent of the legislation appears to be to use
Business and Professions Code section 6036 as both the disqual-
ification and disclosure requirement for attorneys specified in
section 6038. An examination of section 6036 reveals that it
closely follows the general prohibition provisions of the act
(§ 87100 et seq.) including the dollar amounts (§ 87103). Sec-
tion 6036, however, includes its own disclosure requirements
with a special limitation in subdivision (d): "It is suffi-
cient for the purpose of this section that the member indicate
only that he or she has a disqualifying financial or personal
interest." In other words, it is intended that attorney mem-
bers indicated in section 6038 only disclose the existence of a
conflict of interest; they need not identify the specific in-
terest nor report the details required by sections 87206 and
87207.

Concern over excessive disclosure also seems to be the
reason for exempting the council, Commission on Judicial Per-
formance, and State Bar codes from subdivision (c) of section
87302 (§ 87311.5(b)). Subdivision (c) deals with disqualifica-
tion provisions in conflict of interest codes. It does not,
however, require any disclosure. The only reason for exemption
must have been excess caution that no specific disclosure be
required of attorney members of those bodies. Nothing else

3/ Business and Professions Code section 6038 provides:

Attorney members of the Judicial Council, members of the
Commission on Judicial Performance who are not judges, and
employees designated in the Conflict of Interest Code of
the State Bar of California are subject to provisions of
this article with respect to making, participating in the
making, or attempting to influence, governmental decisions
of their respective state agencies other than decisions of
a judicial or quasi-judicial nature.

0406H

explains why attorney members were both relieved from the
disqualification requirements (by § 87311.5(b)) and excluded
from the definition of public official (by § 82048).

Attorney members were not excluded from the definition
of designated employees (§ 82019) which indicates that they
should be included in a code. (See § 82011(e).) Disclosure by
attorney members under the conflict of interest codes for these
bodies, however, should be limited to the information required
by section 6036 of the Business and Professions Code.

This conclusion is based on an overall reading of the
legislation and an examination of the special nature of the
decisions made by members of the bodies mentioned in Business
and Professions Code section 6038. As discussed above, Judi-
cial Council decisions may affect a very broad range of inter-
ests. Yet no decision made by the council within anyone's
memory would have materially affected a private interest of any
council member as distinguished from a significant segment of
the public or the bar.

Amended draft code

I have drafted amendments to sections 2 and 3 of the
council's draft code to reflect the foregoing discussion of
disclosure duties by attorney members under the legislation.
As amended, the draft code requires attorney members to file
annual statements disclosing whether or not they were required
to disqualify themselves during the year. If disqualification
was required, the attorney must identify the decision and re-
port whether he or she complied with subdivision (d) of Busi-
ness and Professions Code section 6036. Judge and legislator
member, who already file full disclosure statements, will have
no additional filing requirements.

The amended draft follows.

Attachments

          All members of the Judicial Council are subject to

Section 2. Disclosure of financial interests
          Members shall disclose an investments, interests in
real property, or and income if during a reporting period a

investment, interest, or income.  The information disclosed
shall be that as required by sections 87206 and 87207 of the
Government Code, except that members subject to article 2.5 of
chapter 4 of division 3 of the Business and Professions Code
shall disclose the information required by section 6036 of the
Business and Professions Code.  This code does not require
disclosure by any member who files a statement under article 2

Section 3.  Contents of disclosure statements and time of
            filing statements and reporting period
(a)  [initial statements]  Each person who is a member on
the date this code is first approved by the Supreme Court shall
file a statement identifying the member within 30 days of
approval.
(b)  [Statements on assuming office]  Each person who
becomes a member after the effective date of this code shall
file a statement identifying the member within 30 days after
taking the oath of office.
(c)  [Annual statements]  Each member shall file an annual

statement by March 1. The statement shall disclose the information required in section 2 for the period following the closing date of the last statement required to be filed.

(d) [Statements after leaving office] Each former member shall file a statement within 30 days after leaving office. The statement shall disclose the information required by section 2 for the period between the closing date of the last statement required to be filed and the date of leaving office.

Each member subject to article 2.5 of chapter 4 of division 3 of the Business and Professions Code shall file annual statements disclosing whether or not the member during the reporting period was required to disqualify himself or herself under Business and Professions Code section 6036 from making or participating in making or attempting to influence any decisions of the Judicial Council. If disqualification was required during the reporting period, the statement shall identify the decisions requiring disqualification and report whether the member complied with subdivision (d) of Business and Professions Code section 6036. Annual statements shall cover the period from February 1 through January 31 and shall be filed by March 1. The first annual statement shall cover the period since assuming office. The final statement shall be filed within 30 days after leaving office and cover the period from the closing date of the most recent statement filed to the date of leaving office.

Section 4. Place of filing statements

Members shall file the statements required by section 2 with the Secretary of the Judicial Council (Administrative Director of the Courts) on forms prescribed by the Fair Political Practices Commission.

Section 5. Assistance to members

A member may request assistance concerning duties under this code from the Fair Political Practices Commission under section 83114 of the Government Code.

0406H

Section 6.  Definitions
       The definitions in the Political Reform Act of 1974,
Government Code section 81000 et seq., are incorporated by
reference in this code.




0406H                            7

1983-1984 REGULAR SESSION                    Ch. 727

POLITICAL REFORM—CONFLICT OF
INTEREST—DISCLOSURE

Senate Bill No. 1427

CHAPTER 727

An act to add Section 6038 to the Business and Professions Code,
and to amend Sections 82011, 82019, 82041, 82048, 82049, and 87200 of,
and to add Section 87311.5 to, the Government Code, relating to the
Political Reform Act.

[Approved by Governor August 23, 1984. Filed with
Secretary of State August 24, 1984.]

LEGISLATIVE COUNSEL'S DIGEST

SB 1427, Petris. Political Reform Act: disclosure: conflict of
interest.
   Under the Political Reform Act of 1974, judges of courts of record
are required to file annual financial disclosure statements, but are
exempted from the conflict-of-interest provisions.
   This bill would extend the conflict of interest exemption to court
commissioners and would subject these commissioners to the
disclosure requirements of the act, as specified.
   This bill would also impose on court personnel other than judges
and commissioners of courts of the judicial branch of government,
the conflict-of-interest and disclosure provisions of the act.
   This bill would impose the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
State Bar of California regarding disqualification and the disclosure
provisions of the act on members of the board of governors,
designated employees of the bar, and attorney members of the
Commission on Judicial Performance.
   Article XIII B of the California Constitution and Sections 2231 and
2234 of the Revenue and Taxation Code require the state to
reimburse local agencies and school districts for certain costs
mandated by the state. Other provisions require the Department of
Finance to review statutes disclaiming these costs and provide, in
certain cases, for making claims to the State Board of Control for
reimbursement.
   This bill would provide that no appropriation is made by this act
for the purpose of making reimbursement pursuant to the
constitutional mandate or Section 2231 or 2234, but would recognize
that local agencies and school districts may pursue their other
available remedies to seek reimbursement for these costs.
   This bill would provide that, notwithstanding Section 2231.5 of the
Revenue and Taxation Code, this act does not contain a repealer, as
required by that section, therefore, the provisions of the act would
remain in effect unless and until they are amended or repealed by
a later enacted act.

The bill would become operative July 1, 1985.

Ch. 727                    STATUTES OF 1984

The people of the State of California do enact as follows:

SECTION 1. Section 6038 is added to the Business and
Professions Code, to read:
6038. Attorney members of the Judicial Council, members of the
Commission on Judicial Performance who are not judges, and
employees designated in the Conflict of Interest Code of the State
Bar of California are subject to provisions of this article with respect
to making, participating in the making, or attempting to influence,
governmental decisions of their respective state agencies other than
decisions of a judicial or quasi-judicial nature.
SEC. 2. Section 82011 of the Government Code is amended to
read:
82011. "Code reviewing body" means all of the following:
(a) The commission, with respect to the Conflict of Interest Code
of a state agency other than an agency in the judicial branch of
government, or any local government agency with jurisdiction in
more than one county;
(b) The board of supervisors, with respect to the Conflict of
Interest Code of any county agency other than the board of
supervisors, or any agency of the judicial branch of government, and
of any local government agency, other than a city agency, with
jurisdiction wholly within the county;
(c) The city council, with respect to the Conflict of Interest Code
of any city agency other than the city council;
(d) The Attorney General, with respect to the Conflict of Interest
Code of the commission.
(e) The Supreme Court or its designee, with respect to the
Conflict of Interest Code of the members of the Judicial Council,
Commission on Judicial Performance, and Board of Governors of the
State Bar of California.
(f) The Board of Governors of the State Bar of California with
respect to the Conflict of Interest Code of the State Bar of California.
(g) The Supreme Court, the Court of Appeal, the Superior Court,
the Municipal Court, with respect to the Conflict of Interest Code of
any agency of the judicial branch of government subject to the
immediate administrative supervision of that court.
(h) The Judicial Council of California, with respect to the Conflict
of Interest Code of any state agency within the judicial branch of
government not included under subdivisions (e), (f), and (g).
SEC. 3. Section 82019 of the Government Code is amended to
read:
82019. "Designated employee" means any officer, employee,
member or consultant of any agency whose position with the agency:
(a) Is exempt from the state civil service system by virtue of
subdivision (a), (c), (d), (e), (f), (g), or (m) of Section 4 of Article
VII of the Constitution, unless the position is elective or solely
secretarial, clerical or manual;
(b) Is elective, other than an elective state office; or
(c) Is designated in a Conflict of Interest Code because the
position entails the making or participation in the making of

388                    Changes or additions in text are indicated by underline

q

1983-1984 REGULAR SESSION          Ch. 727

decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any
financial interest.
(d) Is involved as a state employee at other than a clerical or
ministerial level in the functions of negotiating or signing any
contract awarded through competitive bidding, in making decisions
in conjunction with the competitive bidding process, or in
negotiating, signing, or making decisions on contracts executed
pursuant to Section 10122 of the Public Contract Code.
"Designated employee" does not include an elected state officer
or any unsalaried member of any board or commission which serves
a solely advisory function and also does not include any unsalaried
member of a non-regulating committee, section, commission or
other such entity established by the State Bar of California.
SEC. 4. Section 82041 of the Government Code is amended to
read:
82041. "Local government agency" means a county, city or
district of any kind including school district, or any other local or
regional political subdivision, or any department, division, bureau,
office, board, commission or other agency of the foregoing.
SEC. 5. Section 82048 of the Government Code is amended to
read:
82048. "Public official" means every member, officer, employee
or consultant of a state or local government agency, but does not
include judges and court commissioners in the judicial branch of
government. "Public official" also does not include members of the
Board of Governors and designated employees of the State Bar of
California, members of the Judicial Council, and members of the
Commission on Judicial Performance, provided that they are subject
to the provisions of Article 2.5 (commencing with Section 6035) of
Chapter 4 of Division 3 of the Business and Professions Code as
provided in Section 6038 of that article.
SEC. 6. Section 82049 of the Government Code is amended to
read:
82049. "State agency" means every state office, department,
division, bureau, board and commission, and the Legislature.
SEC. 7. Section 87200 of the Government Code is amended to
read:
87200. This article is applicable to elected state officers, judges
and commissioners of courts of the judicial branch of government,
members of the Public Utilities Commission, members of the State
Energy Resources Conservation and Development Commission,
members of the Fair Political Practices Commission, members of the
California Coastal Commission, members of planning commissions,
members of the board of supervisors, district attorneys and chief
administrative officers of counties, mayors, city managers, chief
administrative officers and members of city councils of cities, and to
candidates for any of these offices at any election.
SEC. 8. Section 87311.5 is added to the Government Code, to
read:
87311.5. (a) Notwithstanding the provisions of Section 87311, the
review of the Conflict of Interest Code of an agency in the judicial

symbol ▽ indicates text deletion                    389

10

Ch. 727        STATUTES OF 1984

branch of government shall not be subject to the provisions of the
Administrative Procedure Act. The review and preparation of
Conflict of Interest Codes by these agencies shall be carried out
under procedures which guarantee to officers, employees, members,
and consultants of the agency and to residents of the jurisdiction
adequate notice and a fair opportunity to present their views.
    (b) Conflict of Interest Codes of the Judicial Council, the
Commission on Judicial Performance, and the Board of Governors
and designated employees of the State Bar of California shall not be
subject to the provisions of subdivision (c) of Section 87302.
    SEC. 9. The Legislature finds and declares that the provisions of
this act further the purposes of the Political Reform Act of 1974
within the meaning of subdivision (a) of Section 81012 of the
Government Code.
    SEC. 10. Notwithstanding Section 6 of Article XIII B of the
California Constitution and Section 2231 or 2234 of the Revenue and
Taxation Code, no appropriation is made by this act for the purpose
of making reimbursement pursuant to these sections. It is
recognized, however, that a local agency or school district may
pursue any remedies to obtain reimbursement available to it under
Chapter 3 (commencing with Section 2201) of Part 4 of Division 1
of the code.
    SEC. 11. Notwithstanding Section 2231.5 of the Revenue and
Taxation Code, this act does not contain a repealer, as required by
that section; therefore, the provisions of this act shall remain in effect
unless and until they are amended or repealed by a later enacted act.
    SEC. 12. The provisions of this act shall not become operative
until July 1, 1985.

_______________


11

BUSINESS AND PROFESSIONS CODE




§ 6036. Disqualification of member for financial or personal interest; exceptions; disclosure

(a) Any member of the board of governors must disqualify himself or herself from making, participating in the making of, or attempting to influence any decisions of the board or a committee of the board if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public or members of the State Bar generally or any significant segment thereof, on:

(1) Any business entity in which the member has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

(2) Any real property in which the member has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

(3) Any source of income including clients, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by or promised to the member within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made; or

(4) Any business entity in which the member is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.

(b) Any member of the board of governors must likewise disqualify himself or herself when there exists a personal nonfinancial interest which will prevent the member from applying disinterested skill and unbiased loyalty to the State Bar in making or participating in the making of decisions.

(c) Notwithstanding subdivisions (a) and (b), no member shall be prevented from making or participating in the making of any decision to the extent his or her participation is legally required for the action or decision to be made. The fact that a member's vote is needed to break a tie does not make his or her participation legally required for the purposes of this section.

(d) A member required to disqualify himself or herself because of a conflict of interest shall (1) immediately disclose the interest, (2) withdraw from any participation in the decision, (3) refrain from attempting to influence another member, and (4) refrain from voting. It is sufficient for the purpose of this section that the member indicate only that he or she has a disqualifying financial or personal interest.

(e) For purposes of this article and unless otherwise specified, "member" means any appointed or elected member of the board of governors.

sections.question: whether the proposed code and subsequent amendments to it comply with the Political Reform Act
sections.conclusion: In summary, it is our opinion that the code as proposed and adopted by the Judicial Council complies with the letter and spirit of the Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the Judicial Council's proposed conflict of interest code, including amendments regarding disclosure requirements for attorney members, comply with the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the Judicial Council's conflict of interest code complies with the Political Reform Act, noting that transactional disclosure for attorney members is appropriate given the varied but unlikely economic interests that could be affected, and that the code properly comports with Business and Professions Code section 6036.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple sections including incoming request letter from Judicial Council (Oct 8, 1985), FPPC response letter (Nov 12, 1985), draft code, and staff memorandum. Primary advice letter is the November 12 response. Clear question and conclusion sections present in the FPPC response.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: whether the proposed code and subsequent amendments to it comply with the Political Reform Act

CONCLUSION: In summary, it is our opinion that the code as proposed and adopted by the Judicial Council complies with the letter and spirit of the Political Reform Act.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission November 12, 1985 Ralph J. Gampell Director Administrative Office of the Courts State Building, Room 3154 350 McAllister Street San Francisco, CA 94102 Re: Conflict of Interest Code for Judicial Council of California Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-85-211 Dear Mr. Gampell: This letter is sent to respond to your request for advice and to confirm my conversation with Ben McClinton of your staff concerning the conflict of interest code for the Judicial Council of California. The code was proposed in response to the adoption of SB 1427 (Ch. 727, Stats. 1984) which amended the Political Reform Act and applied the financial disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act to all judicial agencies.1/ You have asked pursuant to Section 83114(b) whether the proposed code and subsequent amendments to it comply with the political Reform Act. Before I confirm the advice I have given Mr. McClinton on the specific contents of the code, I would like to note that it _____________________ 1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. The relevant provisions for this letter are Government Code Sections 82019, 82048, 82049, 87100-103, and 87300-313. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Ralph J. Gampell November 12, 1985 Page 2 is our view that the Judicial Council may be entitled to request an exemption from the financial disclosure provisions of the Act because the decisions of the Judicial Council do not generally affect specific private financial interests. However, it is our understanding that the Judicial Council does not wish to seek an exemption. In the proposed code, a transactional disclosure category is set forth for the attorney members of the Council.2/ This means that, on their annual financial disclosure statements, these members will certify, under penalty of perjury, that they have not made, or participated in, any Judicial Council decision in which they had a financial interest within the meaning of Sections 87100 and 87103. This form of disclosure is appropriate where the potential economic interests which could be affected are varied and diverse but it is highly unlikely that any one economic interest will be affected. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18733. This reasoning is applicable to the Judicial Council. The code also provides that any disclosure required of the attorney-members by the code shall be made in accordance with Business and Professions Code Section 6036. This comports with Section 82048 as amended by SB 1427 which provides: "Public official" means every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency, but does not include judges and court commissioners in the judicial branch of government. "Public official" also does not include members of the Board of Governors and designated employees of the State Bar of California, members of the Judicial Council, and members of the Commission on Judicial Performance, provided that they are subject to the provisions of Article 2.5 (commencing with
embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter responds to the Judicial Council's request for confirmation that its proposed conflict of interest code complies with the Political Reform Act following the adoption of SB 1427. The FPPC approved the code, including its transactional disclosure requirements for attorney members and exemption provisions for judges and legislators.
